title: Value, reference and const-reference parameters

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ parameter choice communicates copying, borrowing, mutation, optionality, and ownership through values, references, pointers, forwarding references, and smart pointers.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A setter takes std::string by value and moves it into storage, supporting both lvalues and rvalues with one ownership-taking interface. Your goal is to choose and justify parameter forms for scalar and class-type values. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and range iteration; pf abstraction calls; Function contracts and return values. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ parameter choice communicates copying, borrowing, mutation, optionality, and ownership through values, references, pointers, forwarding references, and smart pointers. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Small value types can be cheaper than references, const reference can extend some temporary lifetimes, and forwarding references require constrained perfect-forwarding design. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Passing every object by const reference is always fastest and always communicates the correct ownership contract.

includes: Select parameter passing from ownership, optionality, mutation and cost requirements.
Choose and justify parameter forms for scalar and class-type values.
excludes: Small value types can be cheaper than references, const reference can extend some temporary lifetimes, and forwarding references require constrained perfect-forwarding design.

outcomes: Choose and justify parameter forms for scalar and class-type values.

examples.title: Value, reference and const-reference parameters: defined case
examples.situation: A setter takes std::string by value and moves it into storage, supporting both lvalues and rvalues with one ownership-taking interface.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A setter takes std::string by value and moves it into storage, supporting both lvalues and rvalues with one ownership-taking interface.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Choose by value for ownership or cheap copies and references or views for borrowing; parameter form should express semantics before assumed micro-optimization.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Choose by value for ownership or cheap copies and references or views for borrowing; parameter form should express semantics before assumed micro-optimization.

examples.title: Value, reference and const-reference parameters: boundary case
examples.situation: A read-only algorithm takes std::span<const int> to borrow a bounded sequence without owning or copying it.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A read-only algorithm takes std::span<const int> to borrow a bounded sequence without owning or copying it.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Small value types can be cheaper than references, const reference can extend some temporary lifetimes, and forwarding references require constrained perfect-forwarding design.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Passing every object by const reference is always fastest and always communicates the correct ownership contract.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose and justify parameter forms for scalar and class-type values.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Passing every object by const reference is always fastest and always communicates the correct ownership contract.
misconceptions.correction: Choose by value for ownership or cheap copies and references or views for borrowing; parameter form should express semantics before assumed micro-optimization.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Passing every object by const reference is always fastest and always communicates the correct ownership contract.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Value, reference and const-reference parameters: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Small value types can be cheaper than references, const reference can extend some temporary lifetimes, and forwarding references require constrained perfect-forwarding design.
